--

One of JavaScript’s greatest conveniences is that it manages memory automatically. Unlike languages such as C or C++, you don’t need to manually allocate or free memory. The JavaScript engine takes care of it behind the scenes.
But that doesn’t mean memory management is something you can ignore.
Every variable, object, array, and function occupies memory. If that memory isn’t managed efficiently, applications can become slow, consume excessive RAM, or even crash.
To write performant JavaScript applications, it’s important to understand how memory works under the hood. In this article, we’ll explore the two core memory regions — Stack and Heap — how the Garbage Collector cleans unused memory, and why concepts like closures can sometimes keep memory alive longer than expected.

Two Core Memory Areas in JavaScript
Every JavaScript runtime manages memory using two primary regions:

• Call Stack (Stack Memory)

• Heap Memory
Although they work together, they serve very different purposes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Heap vs. Stack
Call Stack (Stack Memory)
The Call Stack is a highly organized memory structure responsible for managing program execution.
It stores:

• Execution contexts

• Function calls

• Primitive values

• References (memory addresses) to objects stored in the heap
The stack is designed to be:

• Extremely fast

• Automatically managed

• Organized using the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle
Consider this example:
function add(a, b) {
return a + b;
}
add(2, 3);When add() is called:

• A new execution context is pushed onto the Call Stack.

• Parameters a and b are stored inside that context.

• The function executes.

• Once execution completes, the execution context is removed (popped) from the stack.
Because stack memory only exists while functions are actively executing, it is temporary and very efficient.

Heap Memory
While the stack manages execution, the Heap stores the actual data used by your application.
Objects stored in the heap include:

• Objects

• Arrays

• Functions

• Complex data structures
For example:
let obj = {
x: 10
};Here:

• The object { x: 10 } is stored in the Heap.

• The variable obj stored in the Call Stack contains only a reference pointing to that object.
Unlike the stack, heap memory:

• Is much larger

• Grows dynamically as needed

• Is slightly slower to access

• Requires automatic cleanup by the JavaScript engine

Heap vs. Stack — A Simple Mental Model
An easy way to visualize memory is this:

• Stack = Active workspace

• Heap = Storage warehouse
The stack keeps track of what is currently executing.
The heap stores all the data your program creates.
Whenever variables in the stack reference objects in the heap, those objects remain alive.
Once no references remain, the object becomes eligible for garbage collection.
Consider the following code:
const age = 25; // Primitive
const name = "John"; // Primitive
const isStudent = true; // Primitive
const score = null; // Primitive
let salary; // undefinedconst person = {
id: 1,
name: "John",
age: 25
};
const numbers = [10, 20, 30];
function greet() {
return `Hello ${person.name}`;
}
const anotherPerson = person; // Copies the reference
greet();
What is stored in the Call Stack?
The Call Stack stores primitive values, references, and execution contexts.
Global Execution Context
│
├── age → 25
├── name → "John"
├── isStudent → true
├── score → null
├── salary → undefined
├── person ───────────────► Heap Object #1
├── numbers ──────────────► Heap Object #2
├── greet ────────────────► Heap Function
└── anotherPerson ────────► Heap Object #1What is stored in the Heap?
The Heap stores objects, arrays, and functions.
#HeapObject #1
{
id: 1,
name: "John",
age: 25
}
Object #2
[
10,
20,
30
]
#Function
function greet() {
return `Hello ${person.name}`;
}
Key Observations

• Primitive values (number, string, boolean, null, undefined, bigint, symbol) are stored directly in the Call Stack.

• Objects and arrays are stored in the Heap.

• Functions are also stored in the Heap.

• Variables that refer to objects or functions store only a reference (memory address) in the Call Stack.

• Multiple variables can reference the same Heap object.

Why Garbage Collection Is Necessary
Memory is a limited resource.
Imagine if every object your application created stayed in memory forever.
Eventually:

• Memory usage would continuously increase.

• Browsers would become slow.

• Applications would consume excessive RAM.

• Systems could eventually crash.
To prevent this, JavaScript engines automatically reclaim unused memory through Garbage Collection (GC).
Modern engines like V8 continuously monitor memory usage and remove objects that are no longer reachable.
As developers, we never manually free memory — the engine handles it automatically.

The Mark-and-Sweep Garbage Collection Algorithm
Most modern JavaScript engines use a variation of the Mark-and-Sweep algorithm.
It consists of two simple phases.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Mark-and-Sweep Garbage Collection Algorithm
Phase 1 — Mark
The garbage collector begins by identifying root references, such as:

• Global variables

• Variables inside the Call Stack

• Active closures
Starting from these roots, it recursively follows every reference.
Any object that can still be reached is marked as:
✅ Reachable (Alive)
Everything connected to these objects is also marked.
At the end of this phase, the engine knows exactly which objects are still in use.

Phase 2 — Sweep
Once marking is complete, the garbage collector scans the heap again.
Any object that was not marked is considered:

• Unreachable

• No longer needed
These objects are removed from memory, allowing the engine to reclaim the space.
For example:
let a = {
x: 10
};
a = null;Initially, the variable a points to the object in memory.
After assigning null, no references remain.
Since the object is unreachable, it becomes eligible for garbage collection during the next cleanup cycle.

What Makes an Object Reachable?
The garbage collector doesn’t determine whether an object is “useful.”
It only checks whether it is reachable.
An object is considered reachable if:

• A variable references it.

• Another reachable object references it.

• A closure still holds a reference to it.
This distinction is important.
An object may never be used again, but if something still references it, the garbage collector will keep it alive.

Closures and Memory Retention
Closures are one of JavaScript’s most powerful features, but they also influence memory management.
Consider this example:
function outer() {
let x = 10;
return function inner() {
console.log(x);
};
}
const fn = outer();Here’s what happens:

• outer() finishes executing.

• Normally, local variables disappear after a function returns.

• However, inner() still references x.

• Because the returned function is stored in fn, the variable x remains reachable.
As long as fn exists, the value of x cannot be garbage collected.
This behavior is intentional and is what allows closures to preserve state across multiple function calls.
However, if closures unintentionally retain references to large objects that are no longer needed, they can increase memory usage unnecessarily.

Memory Leaks — When Garbage Collection Can’t Help
Many developers assume that automatic garbage collection prevents memory leaks.
It doesn’t.
A memory leak occurs when objects remain reachable even though the application no longer needs them.
Common causes include:

• Large global variables that are never cleared.

• Event listeners that aren’t removed.

• Long-lived closures holding unnecessary data.

• Caches that continue growing indefinitely.
The garbage collector can only remove unreachable objects.
If your code accidentally keeps references alive, the memory remains allocated.

The Complete Memory Lifecycle
Every object in JavaScript follows roughly the same lifecycle:
Object Created
│
▼
Stored in Heap
│
▼
Referenced by Variables
│
▼
References Removed
│
▼
Garbage Collector Marks as Unreachable
│
▼
Sweep Phase Removes Object
│
▼
Memory ReclaimedThis process runs continuously in the background while your application executes.
Most of the time, developers never notice it — until memory usage begins affecting performance.

Key Takeaways
JavaScript’s memory system can be summarized with a few simple principles:

• The Call Stack stores execution contexts, primitive values, and references.

• The Heap stores objects, arrays, functions, and other complex data.

• The Garbage Collector automatically removes unreachable heap objects.

• Modern JavaScript engines primarily use the Mark-and-Sweep algorithm.

• Closures intentionally keep variables alive even after functions return.

• Memory leaks occur when unnecessary references prevent garbage collection.
The most important rule to remember is:
Reachable objects stay. Unreachable objects are eventually collected.

Why This Matters
Understanding JavaScript memory management helps you write faster and more reliable applications.
It enables you to:

• Avoid memory leaks in long-running applications.

• Use closures responsibly without retaining unnecessary data.

• Optimize memory usage for better performance.

• Debug crashes and excessive RAM consumption more effectively.

• Build scalable web applications that remain responsive over time.
Although JavaScript automates memory management, knowing what happens behind the scenes makes you a better developer. The engine does the cleanup for you — but writing memory-efficient code is still your responsibility.
